optionC: Approve changes to project scope.
optionD: Design executive dashboards.
explanation: The executive sponsor owns the project's strategic alignment and budget, and therefore approves changes to scope (which affect both). Status communication (A) is the project manager's job; methodology (B) and dashboards (D) are operational decisions.

question: Universal Containers is about to begin development work on a new project in their Salesforce org that will take many months to complete. UC is concerned about how critical bugs will be addressed for existing live functionality. What is the recommended release management strategy to address this concern?
optionA: Include fixes for critical bugs in the ongoing development sandboxes so that they will be released with the other code.
optionB: Utilize a dedicated Developer Pro sandbox to address critical bugs and release to production.
optionC: Address critical bugs in the development sandboxes and push those changes to production separately.
optionD: Keep teams separate until the end of the project and create a Full Copy sandbox to merge their work then.
explanation: The standard pattern is a dedicated 'hotfix' branch and corresponding sandbox: hotfixes are isolated from in-flight feature work, are deployed straight to production on their own cadence, and are then merged back into the long-running feature branch so the new project picks up the same fix. This is exactly what option B describes.

question: Which statement is true for a Staging sandbox in a typical release pipeline?
optionA: Salesforce major releases (e.g., Winter to Spring) always occur in Staging and Production at the same time.
optionB: The Staging environment can only be updated once every two weeks.
optionC: The Staging sandbox is automatically refreshed on a schedule set by the administrator.
optionD: When created or refreshed, the Staging sandbox is a full replica of Production.
explanation: A Staging sandbox in a typical pipeline is a Full Copy sandbox specifically because it must mirror production data and configuration to provide a realistic final pre-prod environment. There is no auto-refresh on admin schedule (C) or 2-week update lock (B), and during preview windows Staging may be on a different platform release than Production (A).

question: Universal Containers (UC) is implementing a governance framework and has asked the architect to make recommendations regarding release planning. Which two decisions should the architect make when planning for releases? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Whether Salesforce will wait to upgrade the pod until after a UC release is complete.
optionB: When to test a new UC feature release if there are issues.
optionC: How to test existing functionality to ensure no regressions are introduced.
optionD: How to roll back to the previous Salesforce release if there are issues.
explanation: Two release-planning decisions belong to UC: (B) when to retest a feature release if defects are found (in-cycle re-test cadence), and (C) the regression strategy that proves nothing previously working has broken. UC cannot delay Salesforce's pod upgrades (A) or roll Salesforce back (D) — those aren't customer levers.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is working with Salesforce CPQ, which uses configuration sObjects to drive business logic. What are two best-practice recommendations an architect should propose to allow UC to deploy CPQ features as part of their CI/CD process? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Use Data Loader to deploy CSV files.
optionB: Build an Apex framework to deploy CPQ records.
optionC: Use a third-party product (e.g., Prodly, Copado).
optionD: Use an open-source SFDX plugin (e.g., sfdmu) and version control.
explanation: CPQ stores its configuration in records (Product, PriceBook, ProductRule, ProductOption, etc.), not in metadata, so it doesn't move via Metadata API. Best practice is to use purpose-built tooling: (C) third-party reference-data deployers (Prodly, Copado, Gearset) handle CPQ relationship-aware data deploys; (D) open-source SFDX plugins like sfdmu (SFDX Data Move Utility) script the same in CI under version control. Manual CSVs (A) and home-grown Apex (B) don't scale.

question: An architect is working on a Universal Containers project, and due to security concerns they cannot provide the architect with production access. Instead, a central release management team will be responsible for performing production deployments for all development teams. How should an architect leverage the Metadata API to ensure any metadata components necessary to deploy the project's functionality are properly communicated to the release management team?
optionA: Create a change set in each sandbox and download the package.xml file for the release management team.
optionB: Provide a spreadsheet of all components and utilize the Metadata API's readMetadata call.
optionC: Send a package.xml file with associated metadata in a .zip file to the release management team.
optionD: Provide the release management team a copy of the audit trail from the sandbox you wish to deploy from.
explanation: A package.xml manifest plus the corresponding metadata zip is the canonical Metadata API deployable. The release management team can drop the zip into their tooling (Workbench, Ant, SFDX) and deploy it directly into production. Change set XML (A) isn't portable; spreadsheets (B) and audit trails (D) aren't deployable artifacts.

question: Universal Containers has started building a customer Lightning community that contains a few dozen Aura components. The development team lead has come to the Salesforce architect about questions regarding testing the Lightning components. What two knowledge points can the architect pass to the development team lead? Choose 2 answers
optionA: There is a $T test helper object that can be used to create the instance of the Lightning component, and it is promise enabled.
optionB: The testing can be viewed in the lightning.force.com/c/jasminetests.app URL. The page loads and runs Jasmine tests and writes pass/fail information to the screen.
optionC: The testing of the JavaScript part of the Aura component can be tested in the Jest framework and the Apex controllers can be tested using test classes.
optionD: Install the Lightning test service AppExchange package to enable the Aura component testing.
explanation: (A) The $T utility object is the Aura test helper — it provides createComponent(), getComponent(), and other asynchronous helpers, and returns promises so tests can chain steps. (B) Once the Lightning Testing Service (LTS) is installed, you navigate to /c/jasminetests.app (or /c/LightningJasmine.app) to run the suite and view pass/fail results on screen. (C) Jest is the LWC testing framework, not Aura; Aura uses Jasmine/Mocha via LTS. (D) LTS is installed via SFDX CLI/NPM, not the AppExchange.

question: Which two project situations favor an Agile methodology? Choose 2 answers
optionA: A digitization project to update an existing customer-facing process and enable quick adjustments.
optionB: A project with well-defined requirements and complex interactions between front- and back-end systems.
optionC: An environment with a heavy investment in DevOps capabilities for rapid testing and deployment.
optionD: A project to be executed by a third party, with a fixed and formal scope, budget, and timeline.
explanation: (A) A digitization project by nature evolves through user feedback and iterative improvement — Agile's iterative, feedback-driven model fits perfectly. (C) Heavy DevOps investment (CI/CD pipelines, automated tests, fast deployment) is the technical enabler that makes Agile cadences sustainable at pace. (B) Complex, tightly-coupled systems with well-defined requirements are better served by Waterfall's upfront design phase. (D) Fixed scope/budget/timeline is the classic Waterfall contract model.

question: Universal Containers (UC) had added a Service team to the Salesforce Platform. The Service team would like to have a few dozen of the service centers entered into the system as technical reference data. The service centers are made searchable in many different web forms and are rather independent from all other business entities. In the past, they had to manually add any new service centers in each sandbox in the code migration path. They would like to eliminate the manual work if possible. What is an optimal way to accomplish this requirement?
optionA: Add the service centers to a list custom settings.
optionB: Define a brand-new custom object with a picklist field to host all of the service centers.
optionC: Add all of the service centers to a custom metadata type.
optionD: Add the service centers to a hierarchical custom settings.
explanation: Custom Metadata Type records are metadata — they deploy automatically with change sets, packages, and Metadata API, eliminating the manual sandbox population problem entirely. They are also queryable via SOQL, making them searchable across web forms. List custom settings (A) and hierarchical custom settings (D) records are data, not metadata, and require manual population in each environment. A custom object with a picklist (B) also stores records as data, with the same manual-population problem.

question: Universal Containers uses multiple Salesforce orgs for its different lines of business (LOBs). In a recent analysis, the architect found that UC could have a more complete view of its customers by gathering customer data from different orgs. What two options can an architect recommend to accomplish the customer 360-degree view? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Migrate from multi-org to single-org strategy, consolidating customer data in the process.
optionB: Implement a Hub-and-Spoke multi-org strategy by consolidating customer data in a single org, which will be the master of customer data, and using integration strategies to let the LOB orgs read and write from it.
optionC: Implement a Single Package multi-org strategy by developing and deploying to all orgs a managed package which reads and consolidates customer 360-degree view from the different orgs.
optionD: Implement a Complete Graph multi-org strategy by allowing each org to connect directly to every other, reading and writing customer data from the orgs where it has been originally created.
explanation: (B) Hub-and-Spoke centralizes customer master data in one hub org; LOB spoke orgs integrate to it for unified 360 reads/writes — a proven pattern for customer-of-record scenarios. (D) Complete Graph creates peer-to-peer connections between all orgs, letting each org access data where it originated — suitable when ownership stays distributed across orgs. (A) is a valid long-term consolidation path but is a full org merge, not a 360-view integration strategy. (C) A managed package deploys code/metadata but cannot consolidate live runtime data across orgs.

question: Universal Containers (UC) wants to shorten their deployment time to production by controlling which tests to run in production. UC's Architect has suggested that they run only subsets of tests. Which two statements are true regarding running specific tests during deployments? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Specifying the test method is supported in DeployOptions, therefore specify only the test classes that are required to be executed.
optionB: To run a subset of tests, set the Run Specified Tests test level on the DeployOptions object and pass it as an argument to the deploy() call.
optionC: Specify both test classes and individual test methods that are required to be executed, as both are supported in DeployOptions.
optionD: To run a subset of tests, set the RunLocalTests test level on the DeployOptions object and pass it as an argument to the deploy() call.
explanation: (B) RunSpecifiedTests is the correct testLevel enum value for targeted test execution — you pair it with a list of test identifiers in the runTests parameter of the DeployOptions. (C) DeployOptions supports both test class names and individual test methods in ClassName.methodName format, giving you fine-grained control. (A) is misleadingly worded — you can specify test methods, not just classes. (D) RunLocalTests runs all tests in the org that are not from managed packages — the opposite of a subset.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is using Salesforce for their sales organization. The sales users have created several dashboards using multiple running users. The admins have also added a few workflow rules that send email notifications to some sales users. What should an Architect consider while planning the deployment of such components? Choose 2 answers
optionA: If the username in the source org doesn't exist in the target org, the deployment will stop until the usernames are resolved or removed.
optionB: User fields are preserved during metadata deployments and Salesforce attempts to locate a matching user in the target org during deployment.
optionC: If the username in the source org doesn't exist in the target org, the deployment will continue and Salesforce will automatically create the username in the target org.
optionD: User fields are ignored during metadata deployments and all such users need to be manually created in the target org before starting the deployment.
explanation: (B) User references in metadata — dashboard running users and workflow email-alert recipients — are preserved in the component XML and travel with the deployment. Salesforce attempts to resolve each username to a matching user in the target org during deployment validation. (A) If a referenced username does not exist in the target org, the deployment fails with a validation error and must be corrected before it can proceed. Salesforce does not auto-create users (C is false) and does not silently ignore user fields (D is false).

question: Universal Containers has a highly customized Salesforce org, with many different pieces of configuration and code. Which configuration item should be covered by executable tests?
optionA: Workflow Rules
optionB: Case Assignment Rules
optionC: Active Process Builders
optionD: Validation Rules
explanation: Validation Rules fire during DML operations and can be directly exercised in Apex test classes — a test attempts to insert or update a record with invalid data and asserts that the expected validation error is thrown, confirming the rule logic is correct. Workflow Rules (A), Case Assignment Rules (B), and Process Builders (C) are declarative automation that runs server-side; their behavior cannot be directly asserted in the same unit-test manner, making them much harder to cover with executable tests.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is implementing Salesforce and wants the custom code to be unit-tested for all adverse conditions. Which two best practices should an Architect recommend while implementing Test Classes? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Test classes should not create custom setting data.
optionB: Test classes must use existing data in the environment.
optionC: Execute test classes under various profiles.
optionD: Test data must have positive as well as negative data.
explanation: (C) Running tests as users with different profiles validates that field-level security, record access, and permission dependencies behave correctly across roles — essential for enterprise Salesforce implementations. (D) Tests must cover both the happy path (valid inputs producing expected results) and failure paths (invalid inputs, boundary conditions, bulk scenarios) to prove robustness for all adverse conditions. (A) is wrong — test classes should be self-contained and create all needed data, including custom settings. (B) is the opposite of best practice; Apex tests default to SeeAllData=false and must create their own isolated test data.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is planning to move to Salesforce Sales Cloud and retire its homegrown on-premise system. As part of the project, UC will need to migrate 5 million Accounts, 10 million Contacts, and 5 million Leads to Salesforce. Which three areas should be tested as part of data migration? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Account and Lead ownership
optionB: Contact association with correct Account
optionC: Page layout assignments
optionD: Lead assignment
explanation: (A) Record ownership must be verified to ensure every Account and Lead is assigned to the correct Salesforce user post-migration — ownership errors break visibility, sharing, and assignment rules. (B) Contact-to-Account relationships are the most critical structural integrity check in an Account/Contact migration; mis-linked or orphaned Contacts break the data model. (E) Data transformation validation — comparing migrated field values against the source system — confirms all mappings, format conversions, and transformation rules executed correctly. Page layout assignments (C) are metadata configuration, not migration data. Lead assignment rules (D) are rule configuration, not a data integrity concern for the migration itself.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has been on the org development model with scratch orgs already enabled, but they haven't been taking advantage of scratch orgs. Now UC is ready to move to the package development model. What step must be done by an administrator?
optionA: In setup, switch the Enable Unlocked Packages to Enabled, keep the Enable Second-Generation Managed Packages as disabled.
optionB: In setup, switch the Enable Dev Hub to Enabled, then switch the Enable Source Tracking for Scratch Orgs to Enabled.
optionC: In setup, switch the Enable Unlocked Packages and Second-Generation Managed Packages to Enabled.
optionD: In setup, switch both the Enable Dev Hub and Enable 2nd-Generation Managed Packages to Enabled.
explanation: Because scratch orgs are already enabled, Dev Hub is already turned on — scratch orgs are a Dev Hub feature, so that toggle is a prerequisite already met. The remaining step to enable the package development model (unlocked packages specifically) is to activate 'Unlocked Packages and Second-Generation Managed Packages' in Dev Hub setup — a single setting that gates both capabilities. (A) is incomplete because Second-Generation Managed Packages must also be enabled alongside Unlocked Packages. (B) re-enables already-active features. (D) is redundant because Dev Hub is already on.

question: UC has recently acquired companies that have their own Salesforce orgs, merged as new business units. The CEO wants business process standardization among all units, but business process integration is NOT required as units have different customers. Which org strategy should the architect recommend?
optionA: Single-org strategy, as costs increase as the number of orgs go up.
optionB: Multi-org strategy, as it is uncommon for diversified business units to work in the same space.
optionC: Multi-org strategy, as they could deploy a common managed package into all orgs.
optionD: Single-org strategy, as it is easier to standardize business processes in one org.
explanation: When the goal is business process standardization and integration is NOT required, a single-org strategy is ideal (D). One org means one set of processes, rules, and configurations — standardization is enforced by the platform itself rather than replicated across orgs. Multi-org strategies (B, C) add complexity for distribution and maintenance without any integration benefit here. Cost savings (A) is a secondary consideration, not the primary driver.

question: Sales and Service products will be created by two teams using 2nd-generation managed packages. The Sales team needs to use a specific function of the Service product, but only the functions exposed by the Service team. No other team will use these functions. What should an architect recommend?
optionA: Create two 2GP packages with the same namespace; set the methods to be shared with @namespaceAccessible.
optionB: Create two managed packages with Sales and Service namespaces; set the methods with @salesAccessible.
optionC: Create two managed packages, one for each product.
optionD: Create a single 2GP with one namespace exposing all methods.
explanation: @namespaceAccessible is the Salesforce-provided annotation that allows Apex classes and methods to be accessible across packages that share the same namespace in a 2GP context — precisely the mechanism for controlled cross-package exposure within a namespace. Two packages sharing a namespace (A) combined with @namespaceAccessible lets the Service package expose only the intended APIs to the Sales package. @salesAccessible (B) does not exist. Two packages with no namespace sharing (C) have no cross-package access at all. A single package (D) merges distinct products and defeats modularity.

question: What are three advantages of the package development model? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Eliminating the need of using change sets, which should no longer be used.
optionB: Providing its own source control.
optionC: Improving team development and collaboration.
optionD: Facilitating automated testing and continuous integration.
optionE: Significantly reducing the need for manually tracking changes.
explanation: The package development model delivers three core advantages: (C) packages have clear ownership and versioning that improve collaboration between teams; (D) package-based development integrates naturally with CI/CD pipelines — packages can be automatically built, versioned, and installed; (E) package versions replace manual change tracking because the version history is the audit trail. It does NOT eliminate change sets entirely (A) — many orgs still rely on them. Packages also do NOT provide source control (B) — Git or another VCS is still required separately.

question: All AppExchange products are subject to Salesforce security reviews. What is the most common reason AppExchange products fail the security review?
optionA: SOQL injection
optionB: Cross-site scripting
optionC: CRUD/FLS (field-level security)
optionD: Session hacking
explanation: CRUD/FLS violations (C) — failing to check object-level Create/Read/Update/Delete permissions and field-level security before performing DML operations or rendering data — are by far the most common reason AppExchange apps fail Salesforce's security review. Salesforce requires all apps to respect the running user's permissions; code that bypasses this is flagged automatically by the security scanner. SOQL injection (A) and XSS (B) are serious but less pervasive. Session hacking (D) is rarely an issue in managed package code.

question: Universal Containers has multiple development teams working on separate streams with different timelines. Each stream has different releases and delivery dates. What is a suitable branching policy to recommend?
optionA: GitHub flow
optionB: Trunk-based development
optionC: Scratch-org-based development
optionD: Leaf-based development
explanation: GitHub flow is a lightweight, branch-per-feature model where each work stream lives in its own branch, opens a pull request for review, and merges independently — perfectly suited for multiple teams with independent release timelines and delivery dates. Trunk-based development (B) relies on very short-lived branches with constant integration to a single trunk, which is difficult when teams have different release cadences. Scratch-org-based development (C) describes an org provisioning strategy, not a branching policy. Leaf-based development (D) is not an established branching model.

question: Universal Containers (UC) uses Salesforce Performance Edition and is hosting weekly training sessions for 4 weeks (5 days each, new trainees weekly). UC wants to train on samples of production data and delete data at week-end. What is the optimal recommendation?
optionA: Refresh a Partial Copy sandbox every weekend and include an appropriate sandbox template.
optionB: Refresh a Developer Pro sandbox every weekend and include an appropriate sandbox template.
optionC: Refresh a Partial Copy sandbox every weekend and load data needed using Data Loader.
optionD: Refresh a Developer Pro sandbox every weekend and load data needed using Data Loader.
explanation: A Partial Copy sandbox (A) supports sandbox templates, which automatically seed it with a representative subset of production data on refresh — exactly what 'training on samples of production data' requires. Refreshing weekly gives each new cohort a clean environment. Developer Pro sandboxes (B, D) have only 1 GB of data storage and cannot hold meaningful production data samples. Loading data manually via Data Loader (C, D) adds unnecessary work every week when sandbox templates automate that step.

question: Universal Containers uses the org development model and Salesforce CLI. A feature release artifact (.zip file) has been tested in a lower sandbox and is being deployed to a full sandbox for performance testing. Quick deployment options are NOT being used. What is the correct deployment approach?
optionA: Authorize to Full sandbox; Validate with sfdx:source:deploy; Deploy with sfdx:source:deploy.
optionB: Authorize to Full sandbox; Validate with sfdx:mdapi:deploy; Deploy with sfdx:source:deploy.
optionC: Authorize to Full sandbox; Validate with sfdx:mdapi:deploy; Deploy with sfdx:mdapi:deploy.
optionD: Authorize to Full sandbox; Validate with sfdx:source:deploy; Deploy with sfdx:mdapi:deploy.
explanation: A .zip artifact is in Metadata API format (not source format). Both the validation step and the deployment step must therefore use sfdx:mdapi:deploy (C) — you cannot mix mdapi and source-format commands for the same artifact. sfdx:source:deploy (A, B, D) expects source-format directory structure, not a .zip file. Mixing them (B, D) would fail at whichever step uses the wrong command.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has 80% code coverage. Despite having a test plan for each sprint, the number of defects is large. What two items should the architect recommend to reduce defects? Choose 2 answers
optionA: The test script should be used to define the test classes.
optionB: The code coverage should be increased to 95%.
optionC: The test analyst who creates the test plan must also create the test classes.
optionD: The acceptance criteria should have more details.
explanation: The problem is not coverage percentage but test quality and requirements clarity. (A) Using test scripts to drive test class design ensures tests reflect real business scenarios and edge cases — not just lines covered. (D) More detailed acceptance criteria reduce ambiguity for both developers and testers, closing the gap between what was built and what was needed. Simply raising coverage to 95% (B) without improving test design produces more low-quality assertions. Having the same person write both plan and test classes (C) doesn't add independent validation.

question: Universal Containers' architect is documenting the Application Lifecycle Management (ALM) process to communicate it to development teams from different implementation partners. Which three steps apply to any Salesforce development project? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Environment management
optionB: Test or validation
optionC: Continuous integration
optionD: Build release
explanation: Three steps that are universal to any Salesforce ALM process: (A) environment management — provisioning, maintaining, and refreshing the environments used throughout the lifecycle; (B) test or validation — verifying changes work correctly before promoting them; (D) build release — assembling and packaging the artifacts to be deployed. Continuous integration (C) is a practice and tooling choice, not a mandatory ALM phase. Change sets (E) are a specific deployment tool, not an ALM step.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has two full sandboxes — 'Stage' for performance/regression testing and another for UAT. Both were refreshed two months ago. UC is targeting UAT completion in two weeks and a production release in four weeks. A Salesforce major release is scheduled in six weeks. UC needs to release on the current Salesforce version. What should the architect recommend?
optionA: Refresh both sandboxes immediately to get the preview release and complete testing.
optionB: Proceed with the current sandbox versions and release before the Salesforce upgrade.
optionC: Refresh only the UAT sandbox and keep Stage on the current version.
optionD: Delay the release until after the Salesforce major release.
explanation: UC's production release is in four weeks and the Salesforce major release is in six weeks — there is a two-week buffer to deploy on the current platform version before any upgrade occurs. The correct action is to proceed with the existing sandboxes and complete the release on schedule (B). Refreshing now (A, C) would pull in the preview release, requiring re-testing against the new version and likely missing the four-week window. Delaying until after the Salesforce upgrade (D) adds weeks of delay and requires regression testing on the new platform version.

question: Universal Containers (UC) operates globally with regional production orgs for Americas, EMEA, and APAC due to regulatory requirements. Each org has regional customizations AND shared standard features. How should shared metadata be deployed across all regional production orgs?
optionA: Deploy metadata to production orgs using managed packages.
optionB: Deploy metadata to production orgs using unmanaged packages.
optionC: Deploy metadata to production orgs using the package development model.
optionD: Deploy metadata to production orgs using change sets.
explanation: Managed packages (A) are the correct tool for distributing governed, versioned shared functionality across multiple unrelated production orgs globally. They enforce version control, provide upgrade paths, and work across any Salesforce org regardless of instance or relationship. Unmanaged packages (B) do not support controlled upgrades and leave IP unprotected. The package development model (C) is a development methodology/process, not a deployment mechanism. Change sets (D) can only deploy between orgs connected via a deployment connection in the same Salesforce instance — they cannot cross regional production orgs.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has multi-cloud architecture in a single org. The Sales Cloud dev team is working in a Dev Pro sandbox (DevPro1) delivering a release in three months. The business requirements from Service Cloud warrant a quicker release in four weeks, but need part of the Sales Cloud work that is completed in DevPro1. The decision of using a separate Dev Pro sandbox (DevPro2) is still pending. DevPro1 was upgraded to preview for the next Salesforce major release two weeks ago. What should an Architect recommend?
optionA: Clone the DevPro1 sandbox and name it DevPro2 for the second work stream to work on the Service Cloud requirements.
optionB: Push back on the requirements because adding another work stream will bring some risks with it.
optionC: Ask the second work stream team to work on the same DevPro1 sandbox.
optionD: DevPro1 cannot be cloned because it is on a different version from Production. Just create a new DevPro2, and migrate metadata from DevPro1.
explanation: Salesforce supports cloning a sandbox regardless of whether the source is on a preview or non-preview release — the clone inherits the same Salesforce release version as the source sandbox. Cloning DevPro1 into DevPro2 instantly gives the Service Cloud work stream a sandbox that already contains all completed Sales Cloud work, satisfying the dependency without any manual metadata migration. (D) is incorrect — preview sandboxes can be cloned. (C) creates conflicts in a shared single-sandbox environment between two concurrent work streams. (B) is not an architectural recommendation.

question: UC's scale of Salesforce deployment has increased over time, leading to complexities. UC is finding too many bugs in the deployed code, which has become a challenge to the delivery team. The team wants to reduce the amount of bugs by ensuring all the developed code is reviewed, tested, and validated in the upstream deployment process. Which three development practices will be best suited to address UC's concerns? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Use continuous integration with automation testing.
optionB: Encourage the development team to be self-organizing.
optionC: Enable developer teams to do peer code review.
optionD: Incorporate test-driven deployment into the project structure.
optionE: Enable a short and timely feedback loop with customers.
explanation: The three upstream quality gates that directly address reviewing, testing, and validating code before it reaches production are: (A) CI with automated testing — every commit triggers a test run that catches defects immediately; (C) peer code review — a required review step ensures every change is inspected before merging; (D) test-driven deployment (TDD/TDD-style practices) — writing tests first ensures all code is validated by design. Self-organizing teams (B) is an Agile structure concept, not a quality gate, and customer feedback loops (E) operate downstream, after deployment — neither addresses upstream validation.

question: What are three advantages of using the SFDX? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Can store code on a local machine, or a version control system.
optionB: Can quickly deploy metadata using Execute Anonymous.
optionC: Can create scratch orgs.
optionD: Can use native Deployment Rollback Tool to quickly revert to prior state.
optionE: Can install application metadata from a central repository.
explanation: SFDX (Salesforce DX) introduces three key capabilities: (A) source-format development keeps the full metadata source tree on a local machine and in version control — this is the foundational shift from org-based to source-driven development; (C) scratch orgs are ephemeral, version-controlled environments created and destroyed on demand, a core SFDX feature; (E) SFDX can install package versions directly from Salesforce's packaging registry (a central repository) via sfdx force:package:install. Execute Anonymous (B) runs Apex code in an org's context but is not a metadata deployment mechanism. There is no 'native Deployment Rollback Tool' built into the SFDX CLI (D).

question: Which are two key benefits of fully integrating an agile issue tracker with software testing and continuous integration tools? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Developers can see automated test statuses post code commit on a specific user story.
optionB: Developers can collaborate and communicate effectively on specific user stories.
optionC: Developers can observe their team velocity on the burn chart report in the agile tool.
optionD: Developers can use the committed code's build status directly on the user story record.
explanation: When CI tools and the agile tracker are fully integrated, the tracker becomes a live status dashboard: (A) test results from each CI run are posted back to the specific user story that triggered the commit, so developers see pass/fail without leaving the tracker; (D) the build status of the committed code is surfaced directly on the user story record, giving immediate feedback on whether the story's code is green. Collaboration (B) is a general feature of any issue tracker, not a CI-integration benefit. Burn charts (C) are a standard tracker feature unrelated to CI integration.

question: Universal Containers business users often observe that newly released features are resulting in other previously existing and stable functionality being broken. Which approach should an Architect recommend to prevent regression?
optionA: Utilize the developer console to run test suites for the affected functionality.
optionB: Utilize unit and functional test automation as part of a continuous integration strategy.
optionC: Utilize Salesforce Apex Hammer to automatically test all functionality.
optionD: Freeze development of new features and re-architect the system to remove the bugs.
explanation: The only scalable, systematic approach to preventing regression is (B) automated unit and functional tests running on every commit inside a CI pipeline — this catches regressions as soon as they are introduced, before deployment. (A) manually running test suites in the developer console is reactive and doesn't scale across releases. (C) Apex Hammer is Salesforce's internal process for validating platform upgrades; it is not a tool customers control or invoke. (D) freezing features doesn't prevent regression and halts delivery.

question: Universal Containers (UC) deploys major releases on a monthly schedule. In recent months, the team has noticed the deployment time has increased two-fold from 2 hours to 4 hours. The team wants to get back to reasonable deployment times. Which three issues could be affecting their deployment times? Choose 3 answers
optionA: The number and complexity of Apex tests will have a large impact on the deployment time.
optionB: Some components — profiles, custom junction objects, and fields — take longer to process than others.
optionC: The deployments are being scheduled during off-peak hours, which is not the best time.
optionD: Users are working in the org during deployment — locking can affect users and the deployment.
optionE: The team is highly constrained with pre-deployment and post-deployment changes.
explanation: Three proven factors that inflate deployment time: (A) Apex test execution is the dominant variable — more tests, longer test methods, and tests that create heavy data all extend the deployment window significantly; (B) certain metadata types (profiles, complex junction objects, many fields) require more server-side processing time during deployment; (D) active user sessions in the org create record locks that can stall the deployment and cause retries or failures. Scheduling during off-peak hours (C) is actually a best practice that helps rather than hurts — it is not a cause of slow deployments. Pre/post deployment team constraints (E) affect team process but do not affect the platform's deployment duration.

question: What are two things needed to delete metadata with a deploy() call? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Package.xml file.
optionB: The current API version must be used.
optionC: DestructiveChanges.xml file.
optionD: PurgeOnDelete option must be set to TRUE.
explanation: A destructive deployment requires exactly two manifest files: (C) destructiveChanges.xml, which lists the metadata components to delete; and (A) package.xml, which must accompany every Metadata API deployment — for a purely destructive deploy it contains only the API version element (empty components). The API version in package.xml does not have to be the current version (B) — any supported version works. PurgeOnDelete (D) is an optional flag that bypasses the Recycle Bin for records, but it is not required for the deploy() call to succeed.

question: A Salesforce contractor has built an application for Universal Containers (UC). The contractor will need to deploy multiple times from the contractor's own Salesforce to UC's Salesforce environments. Ultimately, UC has full control of the application's code, including its intellectual property. Which deployment method should the contractor use?
optionA: Eclipse IDE.
optionB: Unmanaged Package.
optionC: Change Sets.
optionD: Managed Package.
explanation: An unmanaged package (B) is the right tool: it lets the contractor bundle and repeatedly install the application from their org into UC's orgs, and once installed the components are fully editable by UC — giving UC complete ownership and control of the code and IP. A managed package (D) is the opposite: it protects the ISV's IP by locking components so the subscriber cannot view or modify them. Change sets (C) only work between connected orgs in the same Salesforce instance (sandbox-to-production within the same account), not between two independent orgs. Eclipse IDE (A) is an editor, not a deployment method for cross-org transfer.

question: Which two options should be considered when making production changes in a highly regulated and audited environment? Choose 2 answers
optionA: All changes including hotfixes should be reviewed against security principles.
optionB: Any production change should have explicit stakeholder approval.
optionC: No manual steps should be carried out.
optionD: After deployment, the development team should test and verify functionality in production.
explanation: In a regulated and audited environment, every change — including emergency hotfixes — must be (A) reviewed against security principles to ensure compliance is not compromised, and (B) explicitly approved by the relevant stakeholders to create the paper trail required by auditors. (C) is overly prescriptive; manual steps are sometimes unavoidable and acceptable as long as they are documented. (D) testing in production is an anti-pattern — functional verification should happen in a pre-production environment before release, not after deploying to production.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is working on a major project and has determined that its approach to a certain feature will no longer work with an upcoming Salesforce platform release (e.g., Winter to Spring). What should a Technical Architect recommend to address this issue?
optionA: Continue with the current approach, since Salesforce will rectify the issue prior to updating the production environment to the new platform release.
optionB: Continue development in a non-upgraded sandbox, and have the developer update the API version of the code to the upcoming API version for testing purposes.
optionC: Submit a request to Salesforce to enable the 'delay upgrade' feature in their org. Have the UC administrator schedule the upgrade for a later date.
optionD: Determine the developer sandbox upgrade schedule, and have the developer refactor the approach to the feature in the upgraded sandbox.
explanation: The correct response to an upcoming breaking platform change is to act proactively in the environment where the change is already live: (D) identify which developer sandboxes will receive the new release first (sandbox preview windows precede production by weeks), stand up or use one of those upgraded sandboxes, and refactor the affected feature there so it is validated before the release hits production. (A) waiting for Salesforce to fix it is not a valid strategy — the team owns their custom code. (B) updating the API version number alone doesn't replicate the runtime behaviour of the new release. (C) there is no 'delay upgrade' feature that customers can request for Salesforce's three-times-yearly forced platform upgrades.
